title: Homie The Clown

jokes.text: There's nothing better than a cigarette...unless it's a cigarette lit with a $100 bill.
jokes.setup: Krusty starting to say something positive about cigarettes
jokes.punchline: The absurd escalation of using a $100 bill as a lighter
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Krusty's excess and waste. Strong escalation structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: You know, cigarettes ain't all bad. They're a great way to meet people - just stand outside an office building and hack away!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Plus, they help you save money on food - who needs to eat when you're smoking?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: And if you ever run out of lighters, just use a hundred dollar bill!

jokes.text: Put 5000 bucks on the Lakers. Hire Kenny G to play for me in the elevator.
jokes.punchline: The specific absurdity of Kenny G elevator music
jokes.explanation: Absurd wealth display with specific pop culture reference that feels authentic to the character.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Get me a sandwich, but I want it on Italian bread. And make sure the lettuce is organic. And I want it cut diagonally — no, wait, the other diagonal!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: And while you're at it, play some classy music. Get me that Kenny G guy.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: No, not the album — I want him to follow me around and play the saxophone in every elevator I go into for the rest of my life.

jokes.text: My house is dirty. Buy me a clean one.
jokes.setup: Normal problem of a dirty house
jokes.punchline: Absurd solution showing complete detachment from normal life
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character work. Simple setup with perfect absurd logic that defines Krusty's wealth and laziness.

jokes.text: Did you send 1000 roses to Bea Arthur's grave? - Yes. But she's... - I don't wanna hear the end of sentences.
jokes.setup: Accountant trying to discuss wasteful spending
jokes.punchline: Krusty's complete disinterest in consequences or reality
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Krusty's willful ignorance and the absurdity of sending flowers to a grave of someone who might still be alive.

dialogue.speaker: Accountant
dialogue.line: Mr. Krusty, we need to discuss your wasteful spending. You've spent $50,000 on rubber chickens this month alone.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: So? People love rubber chickens. They're funny!

dialogue.speaker: Accountant
dialogue.line: But sir, you're hemorrhaging money. At this rate, you'll be bankrupt in six months.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Bankrupt? That's crazy talk! Besides, I got a good feeling about this rubber chicken thing. It's gonna be huge!

jokes.text: My 'Seven Words You Can't Say on TV' bit was entirely different from your bit.
jokes.setup: Krusty defending against a George Carlin lawsuit
jokes.punchline: The obvious impossibility of making that bit 'different'
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-reference to famous Carlin bit with good irony about comedy plagiarism.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Your honor, George Carlin's lawyers are suing me because my seven dirty words bit is too similar to his. But I say it's impossible to do that bit any differently. You can't say eight dirty words—there are only seven!

jokes.text: So I'm a thief, am I? Well, excuse me!
jokes.setup: Krusty being accused of stealing material
jokes.punchline: Using Steve Martin's catchphrase while denying theft
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke where Krusty steals Steve Martin's catchphrase while denying he steals material. Perfect ironic structure.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I didn't steal nothing! I'm a wild and crazy guy!

jokes.text: Come on. How about letting me go double or nothing on the opera tonight. - Who do you like? - The tenor.
jokes.setup: Krusty wanting to bet on opera
jokes.punchline: Betting on 'the tenor' like it's a sports team
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about Krusty's gambling addiction extending to inappropriate venues.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I wanna place a bet on the opera!

dialogue.speaker: Bookie
dialogue.line: The opera? What are you betting on?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: The tenor! I got twenty bucks says the tenor takes it!

dialogue.speaker: Bookie
dialogue.line: Takes what? It's not a sporting event, it's a musical performance.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah yeah, whatever. Just give me 3-to-1 odds on the tenor!

jokes.text: Gambling is the finest thing a person can do if he's good at it.
jokes.punchline: The circular logic ignoring that he's terrible at it
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Krusty's delusional self-justification.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey, I'm not a gambling addict, I'm a gambling enthusiast!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: There's a difference - an addict needs help, but an enthusiast? An enthusiast just needs more money to keep gambling!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: And how am I gonna get more money? By gambling! It's the perfect system!

jokes.text: Krusty's Monopoly game, the Krusty Crowd-Control Barrier.
jokes.punchline: Crowd-control barrier as children's merchandise
jokes.explanation: Decent absurdist merchandise joke about inappropriate items for children.

dialogue.speaker: Accountant
dialogue.line: We've got Krusty dolls, Krusty lunch boxes, Krusty backpacks...

dialogue.speaker: Accountant
dialogue.line: And our newest item: Krusty crowd-control barriers!

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Wow, a barrier!

jokes.text: So, what do you think of the Lady Krusty Mustache-Removal System, Angelique? - It's Krusterrific, Johnny Unitas. But is my upper lip supposed to bleed like this? - Probably.
jokes.punchline: Product causing injury with casual dismissal
jokes.explanation: Good infomercial parody with dark humor about dangerous products.

dialogue.speaker: Angelique
dialogue.line: This amazing cream will transform your skin!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny Unitas
dialogue.line: My face is melting off!

dialogue.speaker: Angelique
dialogue.line: That's just the deep cleansing action.

jokes.text: I'll just cut back on the condor-egg omelets. A couple of those would be tasty right now.
jokes.punchline: Immediate contradiction showing he can't actually cut back
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency joke showing Krusty's inability to control himself.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I gotta cut back on expenses. No more fancy stuff for the show.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Starting tomorrow, we're doing everything on a shoestring budget.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: But first, let me call my caviar guy and order a fresh batch for lunch.

jokes.text: It must be the first of the month. New Billboard Day!
jokes.punchline: Treating billboard changes like a holiday
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's simple pleasures and routine.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh boy, oh boy! I can't wait! It's almost time!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The billboard on Route 9! They change it every three months. It's the most exciting day of the year!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not a holiday.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not a holiday?! I've already marked it on the calendar, bought a new shirt, and I'm planning a family picnic to watch it get changed. We'll bring sandwiches, drinks, the whole thing!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, it's just an advertisement.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: JUST an advertisement?! Lisa, last time they changed it from the Duff beer ad to the car dealership ad. I cried. Happy tears.

jokes.text: 'This year, give her English muffins.' Whatever you say, Mr. Billboard.
jokes.punchline: Taking advertising as personal advice and anthropomorphizing it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment showing his literal-mindedness and susceptibility to advertising.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hmm, a billboard that says 'Don't have a cow, man.' Hey, I'm not having a cow. I'm having a homer!

jokes.text: 'Best in the West.' That rhymes.
jokes.punchline: Being impressed by basic advertising rhyme
jokes.explanation: Simple character humor showing Homer's easy entertainment by wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Obey gravity. It's the law! Wow, a law about gravity? Why didn't anyone tell me?

jokes.text: Clown college. You can't eat that.
jokes.setup: Homer dismissing the clown college ad
jokes.punchline: Evaluating everything by whether it's edible
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - dismissing opportunities based on his primary drive (food).

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Clown college? You can't eat a clown college diploma.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose you could try.

jokes.text: I got everything I was supposed to get. I'm not going to enroll in that clown college, though. That advertisement had no effect on me whatsoever.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming the ad didn't affect him
jokes.punchline: The obvious fact that he's clearly been thinking about it
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup for Homer's inevitable decision to attend clown college.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That ad didn't affect me at all.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm just gonna go buy some Duff beer, drive my car into a lake, and grab some donuts... for some reason.

jokes.text: Clowns are funny.
jokes.punchline: Stating the obvious with profound conviction
jokes.explanation: Simple Homer observation delivered with characteristic profundity about the obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, Marge, I've learned something today. If something is hard to do, then it's not worth doing. You just stick with what's easy and hope it works out.

jokes.text: That's it. You people have stood in my way long enough. I'm going to clown college.
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of treating clown college as a noble pursuit being blocked by family
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor treating clown college like a serious life goal being suppressed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going to clown college!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, it's a four-year university!

jokes.text: I don't think any of us expected him to say that.
jokes.punchline: Marge's understated reaction to the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man reaction to Homer's absurd announcement.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're going to clown college?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's right, Marge. I'm gonna be a clown.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Mmm-hmm.

jokes.text: Hold still, Homer. Don't squirm. - I am holding still. I am squirming.
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal compliance with both contradictory commands
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer literalism - following contradictory instructions simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I want you to listen to me very carefully. Do exactly what I say.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: First, go to the store and buy some milk. But don't you dare spend more than five dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Got it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And second, I want you to come straight home. But take the long way around town so you get some exercise.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh-huh.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And Homer, one more thing - be back here in ten minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: All right, all right, I'm going!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm here! I bought the milk for five dollars, I took the long way, AND I'm back in ten minutes!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's impossible. You can't do all three things.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: He invented the pie fight, the pratfall and the seltzer bottle, as far as I know.
jokes.punchline: The qualifier 'as far as I know' undermining his confident claims
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's devotion tempered by honesty about his limited knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Krusty is the greatest comedian who ever lived, as far as I know.

jokes.text: Yeah, Dad. It was bad enough when you tried to pass yourself off as Tom Bosley.
jokes.punchline: Reference to previous failed celebrity impersonation
jokes.explanation: Decent callback establishing Homer's history of celebrity impersonations.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, your impersonation skills are terrible. Remember when you tried to do Ringo Starr and everyone thought you were having a stroke?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That was ONE time!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, you've done it dozens of times. You impersonated Mr. Burns last week and he didn't even recognize himself.

jokes.text: You didn't complain when I got you this close to Chachi. - What's a chachi?
jokes.setup: Homer defending his Tom Bosley success
jokes.punchline: Lisa not knowing the Happy Days reference
jokes.explanation: Good generational humor about pop culture references being lost on kids.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Tom Bosley was the best! He played Mr. Cunningham on Happy Days!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I have no idea what you're talking about.

jokes.text: Those are supposed to be baggy pants. Baggy! - I've never had a pair of pants that fit this well in my life.
jokes.setup: Krusty trying to explain clown costume requirements
jokes.punchline: Homer's pants fitting normally because of his body shape
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific visual humor about Homer's body shape making clown pants fit normally.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: See, a clown's pants gotta be baggy, oversized, ya know? That's the whole bit!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What do you mean? My pants fit me perfectly normal.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, well that's 'cause you're shaped like a clown, ya mook!

jokes.text: Walla Walla, Keokuk, Cucamonga...Seattle. - Stop it. You're killing me. Seattle.
jokes.setup: Krusty listing inherently funny place names
jokes.punchline: Seattle being treated as equally funny
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about what makes place names funny, with Seattle as unexpected punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: You know what's funny? Places with funny names. Like Intercourse, Pennsylvania. Dildo, Newfoundland. Boring, Oregon.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: What about Seattle?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, Seattle's hilarious!

jokes.text: It's a great piece of buffoonery if you pull it off. But if you blow it, you'll look like a fool.
jokes.punchline: The redundancy of warning clowns about looking foolish
jokes.explanation: Good ironic observation about the nature of clowning and looking foolish.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: See, the trick is you gotta warn the other clown, 'Hey, watch out, you're gonna look foolish!' But what's the point? He's a clown! He's supposed to look foolish! That's his whole gig!

jokes.text: 'Kill wealthy dowager.'
jokes.punchline: Misinterpreting stage directions as actual instructions
jokes.explanation: Dark humor combined with Homer's literal interpretation of stage directions.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see here... [squints at paper] 'Homer enters stage left, trips over his feet, and falls into the bushes.' [looks up confused] Stage left? I don't see any stage. [looks around] And why would I trip over my own feet on purpose?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: [reading] 'Homer's face turns red with embarrassment.' [feels his face] Huh, it IS getting warm. [fans himself] This is the worst instruction manual ever.

jokes.text: Burn that seat.
jokes.setup: Someone's poor performance in clown college
jokes.punchline: Extreme overreaction to minor failure
jokes.explanation: Decent dark humor about extreme reactions to failure.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: You flunked clown college? What, you couldn't even make the basic pratfall?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: That's it! You're finished! Washed up! A has-been before you even had a 'been'!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I'm taking away your big shoes, your red nose, your squeaky horn — everything!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: You'll never work in show business again! Not even as an usher at a puppet show!

jokes.text: Get a cheap one, and what happens? Goes off! Takes out the eyeballs of every kid in the room. What's that gonna cost you?
jokes.punchline: Casual discussion of children's eyeball injuries
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about children's entertainment safety with escalating consequences.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Look, these balloons are filled with the cheapest helium we can find. Sure, maybe one in fifty kids loses an eye, but that's a small price to pay for entertainment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Loses an eye? That's terrible!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Homer, Homer, Homer... we're talking about balloons here, not brain surgery. Besides, kids got two eyes!

jokes.text: If there has to be a bastardized version of Krusty, I'm glad it's you.
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment about being the best bad version
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - loving support delivered as insult.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're the best bad father any kid could ask for.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Aww, thanks son!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, you're like the Michael Jordan of being a terrible dad. No one does it better.

jokes.text: Well, it was my major.
jokes.punchline: Homer claiming bank shots as his college major
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist Homer backstory about his educational focus.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, Dad, great shot!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thanks, boy. You know, I majored in bank shots in college.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you didn't go to college.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's what makes it so impressive.

jokes.text: You'll now go back to your hometowns and do kids' parties, swap meets, and the other piddling crap I wouldn't touch with a 10-foot clown pole.
jokes.punchline: Honestly describing their future work as 'piddling crap'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Krusty's disdain for the franchise work.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: You kids are going out into the world to do piddling crap.

jokes.text: Now come and get your catskins...I mean, sheepskins.
jokes.punchline: Slip revealing diplomas might be made from cats
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about cheap diploma materials with good verbal slip structure.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Here's your diploma! Oh wait, there's a slip of paper... 'Warning: diplomas may be made from cats.'

jokes.text: This is the happiest day of my life... Got it. No shock for me. Oh, yeah?
jokes.punchline: Getting shocked by joy buzzer despite trying to avoid it
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy setup with Homer trying to outsmart a prank.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Graduation day! The happiest day of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Dad, shake my hand!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No way, kid. I know a joy buzzer when I see one.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Aw, come on!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not falling for it. I'm too smart for your tricks.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, just shake his hand.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fine, but I'm prepared this time.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Thanks, Dad!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: AHHHHHHHHH!

jokes.text: Got it. No shock for me. - Oh, yeah?
jokes.setup: Homer thinking he avoided the shock
jokes.punchline: Gets shocked anyway as visual gag
jokes.explanation: Standard slapstick gag. Predictable but effective physical comedy setup/payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Whew! I dodged that one!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Ow! Ow!

jokes.text: To audience. I now proclaim this new burger for sale.
jokes.punchline: Reading stage directions aloud instead of performing naturally
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor showing Homer's literal interpretation of performance notes.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to our promotional event! *clears throat* – wait, that's a stage direction. Um, *adjusts tie nervously*. No, that's another one. Okay, let me just... *looks directly at audience*. Dang it!

jokes.text: Homer. Homer, it's all just an act. Stop. Stop. He's already dead.
jokes.punchline: Homer taking the scripted fight too seriously
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy about Homer not understanding performance vs. reality.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why you little!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty Burglar
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, easy there, pal. It's just a show.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A show?! You stole my wallet!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty Burglar
dialogue.line: That's part of the act, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The only act I know is my foot in your—

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.line: Sir, sir, please! You're scaring the children!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll show them what's scary. Come here, you!

jokes.text: Please look at my MedicAlert bracelet.
jokes.punchline: Desperate medical plea during performance
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about medical emergency during children's entertainment.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: Help me! Help me! I'm being murdered!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Shut up, you're ruining the show!

jokes.text: I'm a boy. - That's the spirit. Never give up.
jokes.setup: Homer making balloon animal for wrong gender
jokes.punchline: Encouraging perseverance about gender identity
jokes.explanation: Good misunderstanding humor with Homer's motivational misapplication.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's a giraffe! No wait... it's a poodle! Actually, I have no idea what it is.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: It's beautiful. But is it a boy or a girl?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, that's entirely up to you, little guy. You can be whatever you want to be.

jokes.text: I don't know, Bart. My dad's a pretty big wheel down at the cracker factory.
jokes.punchline: The inherent mundaneness of cracker factory prominence
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Milhouse's innocent pride in mundane achievements.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: My father may work at a cracker factory, but it's an important job!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Important? Milhouse, he makes crackers.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Exactly! Do you know how many people depend on crackers?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I think what Bart's trying to say is that factory work, while necessary, doesn't really have inherent prominence or prestige in society.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Well... yeah, but... my dad's still cool!

jokes.text: But when I see the smiles on their little faces...I just know they're getting ready to jab me with something.
jokes.punchline: Cynical reality about working with children
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke that subverts expected sentimentality with harsh reality.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, working with children has really taught me something important about life.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They're all terrible.

jokes.text: Well, my time's almost up here. So I'd like to say I know Woody Allen.
jokes.punchline: Random celebrity name-drop with no context or purpose
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic - feeling need to impress but having nothing impressive to say. Random but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Random celebrity name-drop with no context or purpose

jokes.text: Let's walk and talk. I have wonderful stories about other famous people that include me in some way.
jokes.punchline: Self-aware description of celebrity name-dropping
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-humor about celebrity networking and name-dropping behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Dick Cavett
dialogue.line: I was just talking to Johnny Carson about this very thing.

dialogue.speaker: Dick Cavett
dialogue.line: Of course, I can't actually remember if it was Johnny or just someone who looked like Johnny.

dialogue.speaker: Dick Cavett
dialogue.line: But that's show business networking for you—half the time you're name-dropping people you may have never actually met.

jokes.text: You're gonna have dinner with Groucho tonight if you don't beat it.
jokes.punchline: Homer's threat implying death/meeting dead Groucho
jokes.explanation: Good threat with clever wordplay about meeting dead celebrities.

dialogue.speaker: Dick Cavett
dialogue.line: You know, I once interviewed Groucho Marx.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Groucho Marx? If I ever meet that guy, I'll kill him!

dialogue.speaker: Dick Cavett
dialogue.line: But Homer, Groucho Marx is dead.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then I'm too late.

jokes.text: Being a clown sucks. Kicked by kids, bit by dogs, and admired by the elderly.
jokes.punchline: Including elderly admiration as equally problematic
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating list with unexpected final item that's equally undesirable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, there's the physical dangers, like getting hit in the face with a pie, or stepping on a rake, or getting your big floppy shoes caught in a door...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And then there's the emotional toll of being a clown. People expect you to be funny all the time, even when you're sad inside.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Plus, elderly people admire you way too much. It's just as problematic as the other stuff.

jokes.text: I'm leaving the clowning business to the other clowns in the clowning business.
jokes.punchline: Redundant phrasing about clown profession
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from Homer's redundant phrasing about professional identity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm quitting the clown profession. It's not a job for clowns.

jokes.text: How could I charge full price to the man whose lust for filthy magazines kept me in business during that first shaky year?
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy giving Krusty discount
jokes.punchline: Crediting pornography purchases with saving his business
jokes.explanation: Good character moment revealing Comic Book Guy's business history and Krusty's habits.

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Your credit card statement shows you've been a loyal customer. In fact, your pornography purchases have single-handedly kept this store in business.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey, I'm a businessman!

jokes.text: Oh, by the way, here is your new issue of Gigantic Asses.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy continuing the conversation
jokes.punchline: Casually handing over obviously titled pornographic magazine
jokes.explanation: Absurd magazine title delivered with casual professionalism.

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Worst. Porn. Ever.

jokes.text: If we agree, then why are we arguing?
jokes.setup: Marge questioning the ethics of the plan
jokes.punchline: Homer's confused logic about agreement and argument
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic showing his confusion about conflict resolution.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I don't think this is ethical.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, Marge, Marge... if something is hard to do, then it's not worth doing. You just stick with what's easy and hope it works out.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But Homer, that doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, a smart guy. Hey, everybody, we got a smart guy over here!

jokes.text: Oh, I thought the Generals were due.
jokes.punchline: Misunderstanding the nature of the Globetrotters' exhibition games
jokes.explanation: Excellent sports humor about misunderstanding exhibition basketball as real competition.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: So I'm watching the Globetrotters play the Washington Generals, right? I figure, how hard can it be? I put ten grand on the Generals.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You bet against the Globetrotters?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I lost! Ten grand! Turns out those guys throw the game on purpose!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's the whole point, Krusty. They're supposed to win.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Well, nobody told me that! I thought the Generals were just having an off night!

jokes.text: He's spinning the ball on his finger. Just take it. Take the ball.
jokes.punchline: Not understanding that it's entertainment, not competition
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up showing Krusty's continued misunderstanding of exhibition games.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Why are they wastin' time with all those fancy dribbles? Just shoot the ball!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: C'mon, ya bums, play some defense! What are youse, a buncha girls?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: This is the worst game I ever seen. They're losin' on purpose!

jokes.text: That game was fixed. They were using a freaking ladder, for God's sake.
jokes.punchline: Describing obvious Globetrotters entertainment as cheating
jokes.explanation: Strong character humor about Krusty's complete misunderstanding of entertainment vs. sport.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Those dirty Globetrotters! I lost fifty grand on that game!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: They said it was entertainment, not cheating. Entertainment? Spinning a ball on your finger while doing the splits is cheating!

jokes.text: So after you finish your performance, you might consider robbing them.
jokes.punchline: Casually recommending robbery of children
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about organized crime suggesting child robbery as business strategy.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Listen, kid. You want to make it in this world? Sometimes you gotta do things that aren't exactly... how do I put this... legal. For instance, I've made a fortune robbing children.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: It's easy money. They don't fight back, they cry a lot, and nobody takes them seriously anyway.

jokes.text: You can't get blood from a turnip.
jokes.punchline: Using cliché while facing actual violent threats
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from using common expression in life-threatening situation.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I'm in deep with the mob, the IRS, and my ex-wives. I owe money to guys who break kneecaps for a living.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: But you know what? I always say, 'When life gives you lemons, make lemonade!'

jokes.text: When he's done in there, I gotta go.
jokes.setup: Krusty asking to use bathroom before being killed
jokes.punchline: Mobster also needing bathroom break during murder
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about mundane needs during dramatic moments.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: Hey, before you kill me, can I use the bathroom?

dialogue.speaker: Legs
dialogue.line: Yeah, sure.

dialogue.speaker: Legs
dialogue.line: Hey, while we're waiting, I gotta go too.

jokes.text: Should I shoot him gangland-style or execution-style? - Listen to your heart.
jokes.punchline: Sentimental advice about following intuition for killing method
jokes.explanation: Brilliant juxtaposition of heartfelt advice with murder methodology. Perfect Fat Tony characterization and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Mob Thug
dialogue.line: So, uh, how should I do this hit?

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Listen to your heart, kid. When it comes to choosing a murder method, you gotta follow your intuition. Don't let anyone tell you different.

jokes.text: Speed holes. They make the car go faster. - Oh, yeah. Speed holes.
jokes.punchline: Homer accepting obviously false explanation
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor with perfect Homer gullibility. 'Speed holes' became a cultural reference.

dialogue.speaker: Car Salesman
dialogue.line: These bullet holes? Oh, those are just... speed holes. They make the car go faster.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, speed holes!

dialogue.speaker: Car Salesman
dialogue.line: Yes, by reducing the car's weight, the engine doesn't have to work as hard.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That makes sense. I'll take it!

jokes.text: I look exactly the same, you moron!
jokes.punchline: Krusty pointing out no change occurred
jokes.explanation: Standard medical incompetence humor with direct character reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Plastic Surgeon
dialogue.line: The surgery was a complete success!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: A success? I look exactly the same!

jokes.text: Lucky I always keep a Bible close to my heart...Lucky I was wearing this extra-large piece of the true cross today.
jokes.setup: Ned surviving bullet with religious protection
jokes.punchline: Escalating to increasingly large religious artifacts
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific humor with good escalation structure for Ned's extreme religiosity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, Ned, you're okay! How did you survive that bullet?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I was protected by the power of the Lord!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's amazing! So God just... stopped the bullet?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Diddly-doodly! In fact, I've decided to increase my faith even more.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: I'm wearing a larger cross now!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's... nice, Ned.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: And now I'm wearing an even bigger cross!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: And now the entire front of my house is a massive crucifix!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, that's getting a little ridiculous.

jokes.text: Actually, my name is Barney. Yeah, Barney Gumble.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to avoid being killed as Krusty
jokes.punchline: Claiming to be Barney, another problematic identity
jokes.explanation: Good escalating panic with Homer choosing another bad false identity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not Homer Simpson! I'm Barney Gumble!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Hey, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Shh! Just go with it.

jokes.text: I cannot do it. To murder a funnyman of such genius would be a crime.
jokes.punchline: Sparing him due to respect for comedy, despite being a murderer
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing mob boss's artistic appreciation. Ironic moral code about comedy vs murder.

dialogue.speaker: Don Vittorio
dialogue.line: You know, Krusty, I'm gonna let you live. I may be a murderer, but I have respect for the arts.

jokes.text: Hey, how about if I squirt you in the face with my boutonnière? Okay.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to avoid doing the dangerous bike trick
jokes.punchline: Offering pathetic alternative trick while facing death
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's desperation and poor negotiation skills.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look, I can't do that bike trick. How about instead I... I'll ride my bike really slowly?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Or I could just... stand here and make motorcycle noises with my mouth?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Vroom vroom... vroom... *whimpers* ...this is as dangerous as it gets.

jokes.text: I'm doing it. I'm doing it. I'm the greatest clown in the world.
jokes.setup: Homer successfully performing the bike trick
jokes.punchline: Immediate overconfidence from one successful attempt
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's tendency toward instant overconfidence.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I'm a stunt driver! I'm going to do it again, but this time blindfolded!

jokes.text: I'm seeing double here. Four Krustys.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony confused by two Krustys
jokes.punchline: Math error in describing double vision
jokes.explanation: Brilliant mathematical wordplay that became one of the most quoted Simpsons lines. Perfect economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. There's two of you?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: No, no, you're seeing double.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Oh! That explains it. Two times one is four.

jokes.text: I can't see, you idiot. Take your hands off my eyes.
jokes.setup: Krusty and Homer performing dangerous bike trick together
jokes.punchline: Basic coordination failure during life-threatening stunt
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy about coordination during dangerous activity.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Alright Homer, on the count of three, we both jump and switch bikes mid-air. One... two...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, switch bikes?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: THREE!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I thought you said THREE means GO, not jump!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: It DOES mean go, ya knucklehead!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then why didn't you jump?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I DID jump, YOU didn't!

jokes.text: Grazie. Grazie. You have brought great joy to this old Italian stereotype.
jokes.punchline: Self-aware acknowledgment of being a stereotype
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor with character acknowledging his own stereotypical nature.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: You know, I appreciate you guys. You're good people. Not like these other mugs who only care about themselves.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: I mean, let's face it - I'm a big fat mobster stereotype. But at least I got principles, you know?

jokes.text: No, no, Don Vittorio. You're not... - Yes, I am. I know it. I am.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to convince Fat Tony he's not a stereotype
jokes.punchline: Fat Tony's self-aware acceptance of his stereotype status
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to meta-humor with character embracing stereotype identity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look, Fat Tony, you're not just some stereotype. You're a real person with depth and complexity.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Homer, I appreciate what you're trying to do here, but let's be honest. I'm a mobster named Fat Tony. I live in a mansion, I have henchmen, and I talk like this. At a certain point, you gotta embrace who you are.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But—

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Besides, the stereotype thing? It's been very good to me.

jokes.text: Forty-eight dollars. - Here's 50. - And 2, your change. And we thank you.
jokes.punchline: Actual amount owed being surprisingly small with polite transaction
jokes.explanation: Good anticlimatic humor about the scale of the 'massive' debt being trivial.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: So, you owe me how much, exactly?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Well, let me see here... *checks papers* ...forty-two dollars and thirty-seven cents.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Forty-two dollars? That's it?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know, I was surprised too.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Well then, here you go. *hands over money politely* Always a pleasure doing business with you, Krusty.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Thanks, Tony. Same time next week?

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: You got it, pal.

tmdb_overview: Krusty the Clown's reckless spending forces him to open a clown college to keep afloat. New graduate Homer discovers the perks and perils of being Krusty.
insight: Homer's clown career spawns 68 jokes in 22 minutes—a dense Season 6 peak driven by character absurdism.
